sentences.base_language: Antoine Martin is a programmer and works a lot during the week.
sentences.target_language: Antoine Martin est programmeur et travaille beaucoup pendant la semaine.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est programmeur** — no article with a profession after **être**: il est **programmeur** (not *un* programmeur). Adding the article changes the meaning to a descriptive clause: c'est **un** programmeur formidable (he's a great programmer).
**Pendant** = during/for (completed duration): pendant **la semaine** (during the week). Compare **depuis** (since/for, ongoing): il travaille depuis lundi (he's been working since Monday — and still is). **Beaucoup** (a lot) follows the verb: il travaille **beaucoup** (not *beaucoup travaille*).

sentences.base_language: On Saturday morning, he woke up at seven.
sentences.target_language: Le samedi matin, il s'est réveillé à sept heures.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est réveillé** — all reflexive verbs use **être** as auxiliary in the passé composé: il **s'est** réveillé (he woke up). With être, the past participle generally agrees with the subject, but when the reflexive pronoun is an indirect object (se dire, se demander), it does not. Here, no agreement marker shows on **réveillé** (masculine singular).
**À sept heures** = at seven o'clock — **à** for time: à huit heures (at eight), à midi (at noon), à minuit (at midnight). For approximate times: vers sept heures (around seven).

sentences.base_language: He was very hungry and wanted to go to the grocery store.
sentences.target_language: Il avait très faim et voulait aller au supermarché.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait** and **voulait** — the **imparfait** (imperfect) expresses ongoing states and desires in the past. Compare with the passé composé (single completed events): il **est allé** (he went) vs il **voulait** aller (he wanted to go — a background desire).
**Avoir faim** = to be hungry, lit. 'to have hunger' — the same for **avoir soif** (to be thirsty), **avoir peur** (to be afraid), **avoir chaud/froid** (to be hot/cold). French uses **avoir** (to have) where English uses 'to be' for these physical sensations.
**Au** = à + le (required contraction before masculine nouns): au **supermarché** (to the supermarket).

sentences.base_language: He washed his face, got dressed, and left home.
sentences.target_language: Il s'est lavé le visage, s'est habillé et est sorti de chez lui.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se laver + body part**: with reflexive verbs and body parts, use the **definite article** (le/la/les), not the possessive: il s'est lavé **le** visage (not *son* visage). The reflexive pronoun already indicates possession: s'est lavé les mains (washed his hands).
**Est sorti** — **sortir** takes **être** in the passé composé (one of the DR and MRS VAN DER TRAMP verbs): il **est** sorti (he went out/left). Compare **partir** (to leave, also être): il est **parti** de chez lui. **Chez lui** = at/from his home — **chez** + person expresses 'at/to the home of': chez moi, chez toi, chez un ami.

sentences.base_language: The grocery store was nearby — only a ten-minute walk.
sentences.target_language: Le supermarché était tout près — à seulement dix minutes à pied.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Était** — imparfait of **être**: for ongoing descriptions and states in the past: le café **était** fermé (the café was closed). Compare: le café **a été** fermé pendant un mois (the café was closed for a month — completed period, passé composé).
**À + distance/time**: **à dix minutes** (ten minutes away) — **à** expresses distance: à deux kilomètres (two km away), à une heure de Paris (an hour from Paris).
**À pied** = on foot — transport expressions use **en** for vehicles: **en** voiture (by car), **en** métro (by subway), **en** vélo (by bike). Exception: **à** pied (foot), **à** vélo (also possible).

sentences.base_language: There he bought eggs, vegetables, and chicken.
sentences.target_language: Là, il a acheté des œufs, des légumes et du poulet.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Partitive articles** express an unspecified quantity: **des** œufs (some eggs), **du** poulet (some chicken — du = de + le). The three partitives: **du** (masc. sing.), **de la** (fem. sing.), **de l'** (before vowel/h), **des** (plural). After negation, all become **de**: il n'a pas acheté **de** viande.
**A acheté** — passé composé with **avoir**: the majority of verbs use avoir. Only être-verbs (movement/state change + all reflexives) use être: est allé, s'est lavé. Knowing which group a verb belongs to is essential.

sentences.base_language: He also grabbed some fruit: apples and bananas.
sentences.target_language: Il a aussi pris quelques fruits : des pommes et des bananes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Quelques** = a few / some: **quelques** fruits (a few pieces of fruit). **Quelques** is always followed by a plural noun. Compare the partitive **des** (unspecified quantity) vs **quelques** (small but countable number).
**A pris** — passé composé of **prendre** (irregular): prendre → pris. Key irregular past participles: **faire** → fait, **avoir** → eu, **être** → été, **venir** → venu, **voir** → vu, **pouvoir** → pu, **vouloir** → voulu.
**Des pommes et des bananes** — in a list, the article repeats before each noun: des **pommes** et des **bananes** (not *des pommes et bananes*).

sentences.base_language: After finishing, he sent his friend a message.
sentences.target_language: Une fois ses courses terminées, il a envoyé un message à son ami.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Une fois + past participle** = once (something) was done: une fois ses courses **terminées** (once his shopping was finished). The past participle agrees with the noun it describes: **terminées** (fem. pl.) agrees with **courses**. This is a concise way to express a completed prior action.
**A envoyé un message à** — the structure for sending: envoyer [something] **à** [someone]. Indirect object with **à**: il a envoyé un message **à** son ami (he sent a message **to** his friend). The message is a direct object (no preposition); the recipient takes **à**.

sentences.base_language: His friend replied: "I'm waiting for you at the café!"
sentences.target_language: Son ami a répondu : « Je t'attends au café ! »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Attendre** = to wait for — takes a **direct object** in French (no preposition): j'attends **quelqu'un** (I'm waiting for someone), je t'attends (I'm waiting for you). Many French verbs of 'expecting/waiting' differ from English: **écouter** (to listen to, no pour), **regarder** (to look at, no à), **payer** (to pay for, no pour).
**« »** — French quotation marks (guillemets), with a space inside. **Je t'attends** — the object pronoun **t'** (te elided before vowel) goes before the verb: je **t'**attends (I wait for you), il **te** regarde (he looks at you).

sentences.base_language: Antoine left the bags at home and went to the café.
sentences.target_language: Antoine a laissé les sacs chez lui et est allé au café.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Laisser** = to leave/leave behind (intentional): il a **laissé** les sacs (he left the bags behind). Compare **partir** (to leave a place): il est **parti** (he left). **Laisser** keeps the object; **partir** focuses on the person leaving.
**Est allé** — **aller** uses **être** in the passé composé: il **est** allé (he went). The past participle **allé** agrees with the subject: elle **est allée**, ils **sont allés**, elles **sont allées**.
**Chez lui** = at his place / at home — chez expresses 'at someone's home or domain': chez moi (at my place), chez le médecin (at the doctor's).

sentences.base_language: His friend Sophie was already sitting there, with a smile on her face.
sentences.target_language: Son amie Sophie était déjà assise là, avec un sourire sur le visage.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Était assise** — être + past participle as adjective: **assise** describes the resulting state of being seated. Compare the passé composé: elle **s'est assise** (she sat down — the action). Similarly: être **debout** (standing), être **couché(e)** (lying down).
**Avec un sourire sur le visage** = with a smile on her face — body-part expressions use the **definite article**: sur **le** visage (not *son* visage). French grammar consistently uses the article rather than the possessive for body parts when the possessor is clear: il a mal **à la** tête (his head hurts), elle s'est coupé **le** doigt (she cut her finger).

sentences.base_language: Sophie asked him: "How are you? You look tired."
sentences.target_language: Sophie lui a demandé : « Comment vas-tu ? Tu as l'air fatigué. »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a demandé** — indirect object **lui** (to him/her): elle **lui** a demandé (she asked him). French distinguishes direct and indirect object pronouns: le/la (direct) vs lui/leur (indirect). 'Ask someone' uses the indirect: demander **à** quelqu'un → lui demander.
**Avoir l'air** + adjective = to look/seem: tu as l'air **fatigué** (you look tired). The adjective can agree with the subject (tu as l'air **fatiguée**, if Sophie addresses a woman) or with **air** (always masculine: l'air **fatigué**). Both are grammatically acceptable in modern French.

sentences.base_language: Antoine sighed and said: "Work has me stressed — there's a lot of pressure."
sentences.target_language: Antoine a soupiré et a dit : « Le travail me stresse — il y a beaucoup de pression. »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me stresse** = stresses me — **stresser** takes a direct object: le travail **me** stresse (work stresses me). The object pronoun precedes the conjugated verb. Pattern: [subject] + [object pronoun] + [verb]: ça **m'**énerve (that annoys me), il **la** regarde (he looks at her).
**Il y a** = there is/there are — invariable, used for both singular and plural: il y a **un** problème, il y a **beaucoup de** problèmes. After **il y a** and other quantity expressions, **de/d'** replaces the article: beaucoup **de** pression (a lot of pressure — not *de la* pression).

sentences.base_language: Sophie said: "You need to relax! Let's go to the park for a little walk."
sentences.target_language: Sophie a dit : « Tu as besoin de te détendre ! Allons nous promener un peu au parc. »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avoir besoin de** + infinitive = to need to: tu as **besoin de** te détendre (you need to relax). **De** before the infinitive is part of the fixed expression — compare: **vouloir** + infinitive (no de): tu veux te détendre. The construction **avoir besoin de** always requires **de**.
**Allons** + infinitive = let's go + verb: **Allons** nous promener (let's go for a walk). The first-person plural imperative is the same as the present indicative form: nous allons → **Allons!** Also: **se promener** = to go for a walk (reflexive — en se promenant = while walking).

sentences.base_language: After finishing their coffees, they went to the park together.
sentences.target_language: Après avoir fini leurs cafés, ils sont allés au parc ensemble.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Après avoir** + past participle = after doing: **après avoir fini** (after finishing). This infinitive perfect construction requires **avoir** for most verbs and **être** for être-verbs: après **être** arrivé(e) (after arriving). It is the standard way to express 'after having done something' with the same subject as the main clause.
**Sont allés** — **aller** + être: ils **sont** allés (they went — masculine plural agreement: **allés**). Note: the past participle with être always agrees in gender and number with the subject: elle **est** allée, elles **sont** allées, ils **sont** allés.

sentences.base_language: The park air was fresh — Antoine took a deep breath and felt much better.
sentences.target_language: L'air du parc était frais — Antoine a respiré profondément et s'est senti beaucoup mieux.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Du** = de + le (partitive/possessive): l'air **du** parc (the park's air — de + le contracted). The two required contractions in French: **au** (à + le) and **du** (de + le). With feminine nouns, no contraction: de **la** salle, à **la** bibliothèque.
**S'est senti** — passé composé of **se sentir**: il **s'est senti** mieux (he felt better). **Beaucoup mieux** = much better — **beaucoup** intensifies the comparative **mieux** (better): beaucoup **mieux** (much better), beaucoup **plus grand** (much bigger). **Mieux** is the comparative of **bien** (well); **meilleur** is the comparative of **bon** (good).

sentences.base_language: They walked through the park for an hour and talked about everything.
sentences.target_language: Ils ont marché dans le parc pendant une heure et ont parlé de tout.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dans le parc** = in/through the park — **dans** for an enclosed or defined space: dans la maison (in the house), dans la rue (in/on the street). Compare **au parc** (at the park — a destination): aller **au** parc vs se promener **dans** le parc.
**Ont parlé de** = talked about: **parler de** + topic: parler **de** tout (talk about everything), parler **de** travail (talk about work). **Tout** = everything: j'ai tout mangé (I ate everything). Note: **tout** as a pronoun is invariable; as an adjective it agrees: **tous** les jours (every day), **toute** la nuit (all night).

sentences.base_language: Walking home, Antoine's body felt a bit sore, but his spirits were very good.
sentences.target_language: En rentrant, le corps d'Antoine était un peu douloureux, mais il était de très bonne humeur.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En + present participle** (gérondif) = while/by doing: **en rentrant** (while going home). This construction is very common: en **marchant** (while walking), en **mangeant** (while eating), en **travaillant** (by working). It always refers to the main subject.
**De bonne humeur** = in a good mood — **être de** + humeur/bonne/mauvaise humeur. Compare: **l'humeur** (mood) — de **bonne** humeur (good mood), de **mauvaise** humeur (bad mood). **Un peu douloureux** = a bit sore: **douloureux** (painful/sore) from **douleur** (pain/ache).

sentences.base_language: He decided to go out with friends every weekend from now on.
sentences.target_language: Il a décidé de sortir avec des amis chaque week-end à partir de maintenant.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Décider de** + infinitive = to decide to: il a décidé **de** sortir (he decided to go out). **De** is required after **décider** — compare other verbs with infinitives: **vouloir** + inf (no de), **essayer de** + inf, **choisir de** + inf, **refuser de** + inf. Knowing which preposition follows each verb is key.
**Chaque** = each/every: chaque **week-end** (every weekend), chaque **jour** (every day). **Chaque** is always singular. Compare **tous les** + plural: tous les jours (every day), tous les week-ends (every weekend). **À partir de maintenant** = from now on — **à partir de** (starting from): à partir de demain (from tomorrow).

sentences.base_language: That night, he fell asleep very quickly and slept wonderfully.
sentences.target_language: Ce soir-là, il s'est endormi très vite et a très bien dormi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est endormi** = fell asleep — **s'endormir** (reflexive, to fall asleep) uses **être**: il **s'est** endormi. Compare **dormir** (to sleep, not reflexive): il **a** bien dormi (he slept well — uses avoir). The reflexive **s'endormir** captures the transition into sleep; **dormir** describes the ongoing state.
**Ce soir-là** = that evening/night — **-là** adds a distancing 'that': ce soir (this evening/tonight) → ce soir-**là** (that evening). Similarly: ce jour-là (that day), à ce moment-là (at that moment). **Très vite** = very quickly: **vite** is an adverb (fast/quickly), no need to add -ment: vite, not *vitement*.
